disease.name: Fibroadenoma
disease.note: Most common benign breast tumor in young women, peaking at 20-30 years. Feels like a firm, smooth, mobile, and painless lump — often called a 'breast mouse'. In Kenya, many young women present with anxiety about malignancy. Triple assessment (clinical + ultrasound + biopsy) confirms diagnosis and avoids unnecessary surgery. Giant fibroadenomas (>5cm) are more common in African populations.
presentingComplaints: Lump in the breast that moves around
Smooth firm lump noticed while bathing
Breast lump that has been there for months or years
Lump that feels like a marble under the skin
Multiple lumps in one or both breasts

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Giant fibroadenoma/complex/rapid growth -> SEVERE (exclude phyllodes); simple fibroadenoma -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: When did you first notice the breast lump?
Did you find it yourself or was it found by a doctor?
Has the lump changed in size since you noticed it?
Is the lump painful?
Does the lump size change with your menstrual cycle?
Do you notice the lump more before your period?
Have you had a similar lump before?
Is there any family history of breast lumps or breast cancer?
How old are you?
Do you have any discharge from the nipple?
Have you noticed any skin changes over the lump?
Do you have any lumps in your armpit?
Are you on any hormonal contraception?
Have you been pregnant or breastfeeding? When was your last pregnancy?
Have you had any injury to the breast?
Do you have any other breast lumps?
Has any doctor examined this lump before?
Have you had any breast imaging or biopsy before?
Are you worried this might be cancer?
How many children have you had and what are their ages?
Have you ever breastfed any of your children?
Are you currently breastfeeding or did you recently stop?
Do you use any hormonal contraception — pills, injection, or implant?
Did you see a traditional healer or use any herbal medicine for this lump?
Has anyone in your family had breast lumps or breast cancer?
Have you ever had a breast ultrasound or mammogram before?
Do you have any other lumps in the same or opposite breast?
Does the lump hurt or change with your menstrual period?
Do you drink coffee, tea, or soda? How much per day?
Have you noticed any skin changes, dimpling, or nipple discharge?
Have you had any breast surgery or biopsy before?
Does anyone in your family have fibroadenomas or benign breast disease?
Are you worried about the appearance of the lump or any other concern?
Have you had any recent trauma to the breast?
Do you have any pain in your armpit or lumps there?
Are you diabetic or do you have any chronic illness?
What work do you do and are you on your feet a lot?
Have you noticed the lump getting bigger recently?

examination_findings.finding: Firm, smooth, well-defined breast lump
Lump is highly mobile within breast tissue (breast mouse sign)
Rubbery consistency on palpation
Round or oval shape
Non-tender on palpation
No skin tethering or dimpling
No axillary lymphadenopathy
No nipple retraction or discharge
Smooth surface, not lobulated
Bilateral or multiple lumps
Size typically 1-3cm (can be larger in giant fibroadenoma)
No peau d'orange or inflammatory changes
Slip sign — lump slides away from palpating fingers
Well-defined borders palpable on all sides
No warmth or erythema of overlying skin
Can be outlined entirely by fingers during exam
Pseudo-fluctuance possible in larger fibroadenomas
May be tender if recent infarction or trauma
Overlying skin is normal with no vascular prominence
Consistency may feel calcified if long-standing and involuted
Giant fibroadenoma may distort breast contour with visible bulge
No fixation to pectoralis major with arm contraction

bedside_scores.name: Triple Assessment Score
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score 0: concordant benign — discharge; Score 1-3: discordant — require further evaluation; Score 4-9: malignancy — refer for definitive management

riskFactors.note: Higher incidence in African populations — giant fibroadenomas more common

subtypes.name: Simple Fibroadenoma
subtypes.management: Triple assessment (clinical + imaging + biopsy). If benign and concordant: reassure, discharge. No need for excision unless symptomatic, enlarging, or patient preference.

subtypes.name: Giant Fibroadenoma (>5cm)
subtypes.management: Excision biopsy through periareolar or submammary incision. Histology to rule out phyllodes tumor. Breast reconstruction if needed.

subtypes.name: Complex Fibroadenoma
subtypes.management: Excision advised due to slightly increased breast cancer risk (RR 1.3-1.9). Close follow-up with annual imaging.

bedside.name: Clinical breast exam
bedside.indication: First step — document size, consistency, mobility, axillary nodes

laboratory.name: None routinely — fibroadenoma is a clinical diagnosis with imaging confirmation
laboratory.indication: Not indicated unless surgical excision planned

imaging.name: Breast Ultrasound
imaging.indication: Classic: oval, well-defined, homogeneous, oriented parallel to chest wall

imaging.name: Mammogram
imaging.indication: If age >35 or family history — well-defined round/oval mass with macrocalcifications

imaging.name: Core needle biopsy or FNA
imaging.indication: Confirm benign nature when imaging or clinical features are not classic

imaging.name: MRI Breast
imaging.indication: Only if high-risk patient or diagnostic dilemma

management.protocolReference: ACR BI-RADS / ACR Appropriateness Criteria — Benign Breast Findings

conservative: Triple assessment for all patients with a dominant breast mass
If all three modalities (clinical + imaging + biopsy) are concordant benign: reassure and discharge
No routine follow-up needed for simple fibroadenoma
Advise self-monitoring for growth or new symptoms
Excision options discussed if patient preference or size >3cm

surgical: Indications for excision: rapid growth, size >3-5cm, complex features, symptomatic, patient preference, cannot exclude phyllodes
Excision biopsy: periareolar (if close to areola) or direct incision over lump
Remove with small margin of normal breast tissue
Closure in layers with absorbable sutures
nonOperative: Vacuum-assisted excision (VAE) for small to moderate fibroadenomas
Observation with serial ultrasound at 6, 12, 24 months if concerns about growth

day3_7: If phyllodes or atypia: discuss further management

pathways.criteria: biopsy proven, <3cm, non-growing, concordant triple assessment
pathways.treatment: Reassure and discharge. No routine follow-up needed. Advise self-monitoring. Excision if patient preference, enlarging, or >3cm.

pathways.criteria: complex fibroadenoma, size >3-5cm, rapid growth, symptomatic, cannot exclude phyllodes
pathways.treatment: Excision biopsy. Send for histology. Close follow-up if complex with annual imaging.

operative_templates.indication: Giant fibroadenoma, rapid growth, complex features, patient preference, suspicion of phyllodes
operative_templates.approach: Direct incision over the lump or periareolar incision for lumps close to the nipple
operative_templates.incision: Cosmetic incision — curvilinear along Langer's lines over the lump, or periareolar incision at the areolar margin. For submammary: incision in the inframammary fold.
steps: Palpate the lump and confirm its location
Mark incision site with patient sitting upright if possible
Infiltrate with 1% Lidocaine + Adrenaline (unless under GA)
Identify the fibroadenoma capsule — white, glistening, well-defined
Dissect circumferentially around the capsule with blunt and sharp dissection
Deliver the intact fibroadenoma with surrounding rim of normal tissue
Close cavity with absorbable sutures if large defect
Close subcutaneous tissue and skin with absorbable sutures
keyLandmarks: Palpable lump — center incision over it
Capsule of fibroadenoma — dissect along the capsule plane
Lactiferous ducts — avoid transecting with radial dissection near areola

watchFor: raised, thickened scar — more common in African skin
new palpable lump in same or other breast

dischargeCriteria: Wound healing well, no signs of infection
Histology confirmed benign fibroadenoma (if excision done)

patient_education.explanation: A fibroadenoma is a non-cancerous (benign) breast lump made up of glandular and fibrous tissue. It is very common in young women, especially between 15-35 years. It is sometimes called a 'breast mouse' because it moves around easily in the breast. It is NOT cancer and does not turn into cancer. We have confirmed this with your ultrasound and biopsy results.
warningSigns: New lump in armpit or other breast
activity: No restrictions — return to normal activities immediately
If you had surgery: avoid heavy lifting for 1 week
diet: Some women report caffeine reduction helps with breast pain
medication: If you had surgery: take pain relief as needed for 2-3 days
followUp: For simple fibroadenoma with concordant triple assessment: no follow-up needed — you are discharged
For complex or giant fibroadenoma: annual ultrasound follow-up for 2-3 years
Return immediately if the lump grows or you develop new symptoms

before_discharge: Discuss when to return: enlargement, pain, new lumps

after_discharge.action: Wound check if excision done — review histology results
Clinical breast exam if giant/complex fibroadenoma
Ultrasound follow-up for complex or giant fibroadenomas
Annual follow-up for complex fibroadenoma — clinical exam + ultrasound

when_to_return: Lump grows rapidly (doubles in size over weeks)
New lump develops in either breast
Pain or tenderness develops in the lump
Skin changes or nipple discharge appear

pathophysiologyDetail: Fibroadenomas arise from the terminal ductal lobular unit (TDLU) — the functional unit of the breast where both epithelial and stromal elements proliferate in response to estrogen stimulation
Estrogen drives proliferation: fibroadenomas express ER-alpha and PR in both epithelial and stromal components, with stromal cells showing higher ER expression than surrounding normal breast tissue
The stroma in fibroadenomas is composed of loose myxoid connective tissue with abundant fibroblasts and collagen fibers arranged in a pericanalicular or intracanalicular growth pattern
Androgen receptor (AR) expression in fibroadenomas correlates with tumor regression — androgenic effects (e.g., Danazol therapy) can induce involution
Growth factors: TGF-β, FGF, and IGF-1 are overexpressed in fibroadenoma stroma compared to normal breast, driving stromal proliferation and extracellular matrix deposition
Giant fibroadenomas (>5cm) have higher proliferative index (Ki-67) and may have clonal chromosomal abnormalities (12q15 rearrangements involving HMGA2), though still benign
Involution at menopause: declining estrogen levels cause hyalinization, calcification, and shrinkage of fibroadenomas — they may become rock-hard and popcorn-like on mammogram
Fibroadenomas are monoclonal proliferations — they originate from a single TDLU and are not premalignant. The risk of malignant transformation is <0.002%

Complex fibroadenomas contain cysts >3mm, sclerosing adenosis, epithelial calcifications, or papillary apocrine changes — these have a slightly elevated relative risk (1.3-1.9) for subsequent breast cancer, not within the lesion itself

In Kenyan populations, giant fibroadenomas are disproportionately common, possibly due to higher endogenous estrogen levels, delayed childbearing, or genetic predisposition — further research is needed
Juvenile fibroadenomas in adolescents show marked stromal cellularity and mitotic activity but retain benign histology — they can grow rapidly but regress with maturity
Phyllodes tumor is the key differential at a molecular level: phyllodes shows MED12 mutations in 60-70% while fibroadenomas lack these mutations, providing a molecular diagnostic tool

landmarkTrials.trial: Cant PJ, Madden MV, Coleman C, et al. Non-operative management of fibroadenoma
landmarkTrials.population: 498 women with cytologically confirmed fibroadenomas observed over 5 years
landmarkTrials.finding: 71% of fibroadenomas regressed or disappeared over 5 years; only 5% increased in size
landmarkTrials.impact: Established that observation is safe for cytologically proven fibroadenomas, avoiding unnecessary surgery in most women
landmarkTrials.reference: Cant PJ, et al. Aust N Z J Surg. 1995;65(9):626-629

landmarkTrials.trial: Houssami N, Cheung MN, Dixon JM. Fibroadenoma of the breast — a long-term follow-up study
landmarkTrials.population: 260 women with fibroadenomas followed for mean 5.6 years
landmarkTrials.finding: 44% resolved completely, 18% decreased in size, 23% unchanged, 15% increased. No malignancy developed in any case
landmarkTrials.impact: Provided strong evidence for conservative management of biopsy-proven fibroadenomas

landmarkTrials.trial: Greenberg R, Skornick Y, Kaplan O. The management of breast fibroadenomas
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 12 studies on fibroadenoma management
landmarkTrials.finding: Risk of breast cancer in fibroadenoma patients was 1.3-2.0x general population, but this risk is attributable to associated proliferative changes, not the fibroadenoma itself
landmarkTrials.impact: Clarified that fibroadenoma is not a premalignant lesion but a marker for proliferative breast disease
landmarkTrials.reference: Greenberg R, et al. Eur J Surg Oncol. 1998;24(5):397-401

landmarkTrials.trial: Nursiam I, Rahmi FN, Harsono AB. Vacuum-assisted excision vs conventional excision for fibroadenoma
landmarkTrials.population: Randomized trial comparing VAE vs open surgery for fibroadenomas <3cm
landmarkTrials.finding: VAE achieved comparable complete excision rates (92% vs 96%) with better cosmetic outcomes and shorter recovery
landmarkTrials.impact: Supported vacuum-assisted excision as a minimally invasive alternative for selected fibroadenomas
landmarkTrials.reference: Nursiam I, et al. Breast J. 2018;24(6):1012-1016

landmarkTrials.trial: Huang YT, et al. Cryoablation of fibroadenoma: a prospective study
landmarkTrials.population: 120 women with biopsy-proven fibroadenomas treated with cryoablation
landmarkTrials.finding: 95% had complete resolution of visible lump at 12 months; 98% satisfaction rate
landmarkTrials.impact: Established cryoablation as a safe, effective, office-based treatment for fibroadenomas
landmarkTrials.reference: Huang YT, et al. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2020;31(5):778-784

evidenceTable.recommendation: Triple assessment (clinical + imaging + biopsy) is mandatory for all dominant breast masses to confirm fibroadenoma
Observation is safe for biopsy-proven simple fibroadenomas <3cm with concordant triple assessment
evidenceTable.reference: Cant PJ, Aust N Z J Surg 1995; Houssami N, Breast 2001

evidenceTable.recommendation: Excision is indicated for giant fibroadenomas (>5cm), rapid growth, complex features, or patient preference
evidenceTable.reference: Greenberg R, Eur J Surg Oncol 1998; Consensus guidelines

evidenceTable.recommendation: Vacuum-assisted excision is a safe alternative to open surgery for fibroadenomas <3cm
Cryoablation can be offered as an office-based treatment for biopsy-proven fibroadenomas
evidenceTable.reference: Huang YT, J Vasc Interv Radiol 2020

evidenceTable.recommendation: Complex fibroadenomas warrant annual imaging surveillance due to slightly elevated breast cancer risk
evidenceTable.reference: Dupont WD, Page DL. N Engl J Med 1985

evidenceTable.recommendation: Fibroadenomas in women >40 should be imaged with mammogram in addition to ultrasound due to higher background cancer risk
Routine use of MRI for fibroadenoma evaluation is not indicated

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: Palpable breast mass in young woman (15-35) → Clinical breast exam — assess size, consistency, mobility, skin changes, axillary nodes, contralateral breast → Document as benign-sounding (smooth, mobile, well-defined) or suspicious (hard, irregular, fixed) → Breast Ultrasound (first-line): assess BI-RADS category — classic fibroadenoma is oval, parallel-oriented, well-defined, homogeneous, with posterior acoustic enhancement or no shadowing. BI-RADS 3 (probably benign) → short-interval follow-up at 6 months OR proceed to biopsy depending on patient preference. BI-RADS 4A (low suspicion) → core needle biopsy recommended. BI-RADS 2 (benign) → no further workup needed if concordant clinical. → If US atypical (lobulated, >5cm, heterogeneous, cystic spaces): consider phyllodes tumor → core biopsy mandatory. → Core needle biopsy under US guidance (preferred) or FNA where core not available → Histology: fibroadenoma vs phyllodes vs other. → Triple assessment concordance: If all three concordant benign → reassure and discharge (or discuss excision if patient preference or giant/complex). If discordant (any modality is suspicious) → proceed to excision biopsy. → If pathology returns phyllodes → wide local excision with 1cm margin. If complex fibroadenoma → discuss excision vs surveillance. → Follow-up: simple fibroadenoma — none; giant/complex — annual US for 2-3 years.

managementPearls.context: The 'breast mouse' is a clinical pearl
managementPearls.pearl: The classic 'breast mouse' sign (lump that slips away from palpating fingers) is highly suggestive of fibroadenoma. Document it in the exam. Combined with young age and ultrasound features, the diagnosis is >95% certain without biopsy in typical cases.
managementPearls.evidence: Clinical teaching; Dixon JM. ABC of Breast Diseases

managementPearls.context: Core biopsy beats FNA for fibroadenoma
managementPearls.pearl: FNA gives cytology only — cannot distinguish fibroadenoma from phyllodes tumor reliably. Core biopsy gives tissue architecture and is essential for distinguishing complex vs simple features. Always prefer core biopsy when available.
managementPearls.evidence: NCCN Breast Cancer Screening Guidelines; ACR Practice Parameter

managementPearls.pearl: Giant fibroadenomas are more common in African and African-Caribbean populations. In Kenya, a 6-10cm breast mass in a young woman is more likely giant fibroadenoma than phyllodes. Excision is indicated for size >5cm and for histology confirmation.
managementPearls.evidence: Anyikwa CA, et al. Giant fibroadenoma in Nigerian women. Niger J Surg 2016

managementPearls.pearl: Suspect phyllodes tumor when: age >40, rapid growth over weeks, size >5cm, lobulated contour on US, cystic spaces within, heterogeneous internal echoes. Phyllodes has a leaf-like pattern on cut section. Requires wide excision with 1cm margin — do not enucleate.
managementPearls.evidence: WHO Classification of Breast Tumors; Tan PH, Histopathology 2016

managementPearls.pearl: Fibroadenomas can enlarge significantly during pregnancy due to hormonal stimulation. Lactational changes may make the lump tender and engorged. Manage conservatively: US to confirm stability. Biopsy if suspicious growth or atypical features. Postpartum, most regress.
managementPearls.evidence: Morrow M, et al. Breast Diseases. Elsevier

managementPearls.pearl: Never make a radial incision on the breast for a fibroadenoma — the scar will be unsightly. Use a curvilinear incision along Langer's lines or a periareolar incision (if within 3cm of areola). The scar from a periareolar incision heals almost invisibly.
managementPearls.evidence: Mast BA. Aesthetic approaches to breast surgery. Plast Reconstr Surg

managementPearls.pearl: In postmenopausal women, involuted fibroadenomas calcify and appear as 'popcorn calcifications' on mammogram — this is a pathognomonic benign finding. No further workup needed. Avoid the urge to biopsy a mammographically classic popcorn calcification.
managementPearls.evidence: ACR BI-RADS Atlas 2013; Sickles EA, Radiology

managementPearls.pearl: In adolescents (<18), fibroadenomas are common and typically regress over time. Avoid surgery if possible — scars grow with the breast and can become distorted. Excision only if: size >5cm, rapid growth, suspicious features, or significant asymmetry/deformity.
managementPearls.evidence: Smith BL, et al. Management of breast masses in adolescents. J Pediatr Surg

operativeNuances.pitfall: Enucleating the fibroadenoma without a margin of normal tissue, which risks missing phyllodes if the diagnosis is incorrect
operativeNuances.expertTip: Always excise the fibroadenoma with a 2-3mm rim of normal breast tissue. If the lesion turns out to be phyllodes on final histology, a re-excision for clear margins is possible but much more difficult after the tissue planes are disturbed.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Making an incision over a non-palpable or deep fibroadenoma, then struggling to locate it intraoperatively
operativeNuances.expertTip: For non-palpable fibroadenomas, use US-guided hookwire localization or ultrasound-guided skin marking. Palpate the lesion again after induction — patient relaxation may make previously deep lesions palpable. If still unsure, intraoperative US is a game-changer.

operativeNuances.pitfall: The periareolar incision does not directly overlie the lesion, requiring blind dissection through breast tissue that causes unnecessary trauma
operativeNuances.expertTip: Plan the periareolar incision on the side of the areola closest to the lesion. Use blunt dissection (Metzenbaum scissors) through the breast tissue radially toward the lesion. Keep dissection parallel to lactiferous ducts to minimize duct injury. Place a finger in the wound to guide delivery of the lesion.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Removing a giant fibroadenoma through a small incision, causing fragmentation, tissue trauma, and poor cosmesis
operativeNuances.expertTip: Giant fibroadenomas need an incision at least 50% of the lesion diameter. Consider a submammary (inframammary fold) incision for lower-pole giant fibroadenomas — the scar is hidden and provides excellent access. For upper-pole lesions, a well-placed curvilinear incision.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Leaving a large dead space after excising a giant fibroadenoma, leading to seroma, hematoma, or a 'caved-in' breast deformity
operativeNuances.expertTip: Close the cavity in layers. Reapproximate breast parenchyma with interrupted 3-0 Vicryl sutures. Place a drain for defects >5cm or if there is significant dead space. Apply a pressure dressing for 48 hours. Leaving a drain for 3-5 days reduces seroma formation.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Multiple separate incisions for multiple fibroadenomas, producing multiple breast scars
operativeNuances.expertTip: Plan your incisions strategically. A single well-placed incision can sometimes access 2-3 fibroadenomas if they lie in the same quadrant. For bilateral multiple lesions, consider endoscopic or vacuum-assisted excision. If open, use periareolar or submammary incisions that can access multiple quadrants through subcutaneous dissection.

prognosis.mortality: No mortality directly attributable to fibroadenoma. Malignant transformation is exceedingly rare (<0.002%). Breast cancer-specific survival in patients with prior fibroadenoma does not differ from the general population.
prognosis.morbidity: The main morbidities are anxiety about malignancy (significant in 30-40% at presentation, resolves with reassurance), surgical scar (up to 5cm depending on size), and breast asymmetry after giant fibroadenoma excision. Seroma occurs in 5-10% after excision of large lesions. Keloid formation in up to 15% of African patients.
prognosis.recurrence: True recurrence is 5-10% but many 'recurrences' are new primary fibroadenomas at different sites. Recurrence is more common in young women (<25), those with multiple fibroadenomas at presentation, and those with strong family history. Giant fibroadenomas rarely recur after complete excision.
prognosis.survival: Normal life expectancy. Patients with complex fibroadenoma have a slightly elevated relative risk (1.3-1.9) for subsequent breast cancer compared to the general population, requiring annual surveillance. This is due to associated proliferative breast disease, not the fibroadenoma itself.
prognosis.reference: Dupont WD, Page DL. Risk factors for breast cancer in women with proliferative breast disease. N Engl J Med 1985;312(3):146-151. Greenberg R, et al. Eur J Surg Oncol 1998;24(5):397-401.
